questions.optionB: Clostridium perfringens (anaerobic, spore-forming — produces α-toxin/lecithinase causing myonecrosis)
questions.explanation: Gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis): C. perfringens (most common, 80-95%). α-toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C): destroys cell membranes → myonecrosis. Features: severe pain, crepitus (gas in tissues on palpation/XR), dishwater/serosanguinous discharge, rapid progression, toxemia. Surgical emergency: radical debridement + high-dose penicillin + clindamycin (reduces toxin production). Hyperbaric oxygen: adjunctive. Nagler reaction: α-toxin detection (lecithinase on egg yolk agar inhibited by antitoxin). Reference: Bailey & Love — Gas Gangrene.

questions.questionText: Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency requiring:
questions.optionB: Urgent surgical debridement + broad-spectrum antibiotics (rapidly progressive infection of fascia with high mortality)
questions.explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis: rapidly progressive infection along fascial planes → necrosis of subcutaneous tissue and fascia. Type I: polymicrobial (mixed aerobic-anaerobic). Type II: Group A Strep (S. pyogenes) — 'flesh-eating bacteria', or S. aureus. Signs: pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading erythema, crepitus, skin necrosis, systemic toxicity (septic shock). 'Dishwasher fluid' discharge. Treatment: EMERGENCY wide surgical debridement (lifesaving) + IV antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem). Mortality: 20-40% even with treatment. Reference: Bailey & Love — Necrotizing Fasciitis.

questions.questionText: Surgical site infection (SSI) prophylaxis antibiotics should ideally be given:
questions.optionB: Within 60 minutes before skin incision (30 min for vancomycin/fluoroquinolones) — to achieve adequate tissue levels
questions.explanation: SSI prophylaxis: antibiotic given within 60 minutes before incision (to achieve therapeutic tissue concentration at time of contamination). Cefazolin: most common choice (clean/clean-contaminated). Redose: if surgery >3 hours or blood loss >1.5L. Duration: single dose (or ≤24 hours post-op). NOT required for clean procedures with low risk (except implants/prosthetics). Metronidazole: added for colorectal surgery (anaerobic coverage). Reference: Bailey & Love — SSI Prophylaxis.

questions.questionText: Ludwig's angina is an infection of:
questions.optionB: Bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces (rapidly progressive cellulitis — can cause airway obstruction)
questions.explanation: Ludwig's angina: severe bilateral cellulitis/infection of submandibular space (submandibular, sublingual, submental). Usually from dental infection (2nd/3rd mandibular molars — roots below mylohyoid line). Features: 'woody' induration of floor of mouth, tongue elevation/protrusion, drooling, dysphagia, stridor. Life-threatening: airway obstruction (edema pushes tongue posterosuperiorly). Management: secure airway FIRST (may need tracheostomy), IV antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or ampicillin-sulbactam), surgical drainage if abscess. Reference: Bailey & Love — Ludwig's Angina.
